chapters.title: Motion
notes: Motion: Motion is the change in the position of an object with respect to its surroundings over time. An object is said to be in motion when its position changes relative to a reference point.
Distance: Distance is the total length of the actual path travelled by an object during motion. It is a scalar quantity and is always positive.
Displacement: Displacement is the shortest straight-line distance between the initial and final positions of an object. It is a vector quantity because it has both magnitude and direction.
Speed: Speed is the distance travelled by an object in unit time. It is a scalar quantity and does not depend on the direction of motion.
Velocity: Velocity is the displacement covered by an object in unit time. It is a vector quantity because both magnitude and direction are important.
Difference Between Speed and Velocity: Speed depends only on the distance travelled by an object. Velocity depends on both displacement and the direction of motion.
Uniform Motion: An object is said to be in uniform motion when it covers equal distances in equal intervals of time. Its speed remains constant throughout the motion.
Non-uniform Motion: An object is in non-uniform motion when it covers unequal distances in equal intervals of time. Its speed or direction changes during motion.
Acceleration: Acceleration is the rate of change of velocity with time. It can occur when the speed, direction, or both change.

Positive, Negative and Zero Acceleration: Positive acceleration occurs when the velocity of an object increases with time. Negative acceleration (deceleration) occurs when velocity decreases, while zero acceleration means the velocity remains constant.

Distance-Time Graph: A distance-time graph shows how the distance travelled by an object changes with time. A straight line represents uniform motion, while a curved line represents non-uniform motion.
Velocity-Time Graph: A velocity-time graph shows how the velocity of an object changes with time. The area under the graph represents the distance travelled during that time.
Equations of Motion: The three equations of motion are — v = u + at | s = ut + ½at² | v² = u² + 2as, where u = initial velocity, v = final velocity, a = acceleration, t = time and s = displacement.

Uniform Circular Motion: Uniform circular motion is the motion of an object along a circular path with constant speed. Although the speed remains constant, the velocity changes continuously because the direction changes at every point.

Newton's First Law of Motion: Newton's First Law states that an object remains at rest or continues to move with uniform velocity in a straight line unless acted upon by an external unbalanced force. This law is also called the Law of Inertia.
Inertia: Inertia is the property of an object to resist any change in its state of rest or motion. Objects with greater mass have greater inertia.

Balanced and Unbalanced Forces: Balanced forces are equal in magnitude and opposite in direction, so they do not change the motion of an object. Unbalanced forces produce a net force that changes the speed or direction of motion.

Newton's Second Law of Motion: Newton's Second Law states that the rate of change of momentum is directly proportional to the applied force and takes place in the direction of the force. Formula: F = ma, where F = Force, m = Mass, a = Acceleration.
Momentum: Momentum is the product of the mass and velocity of an object. It is a vector quantity and its direction is the same as the direction of velocity. Formula: P = m × v.
SI Unit of Force: The SI unit of force is Newton (N). One Newton is the force required to produce an acceleration of 1 m/s² in a body of mass 1 kg.
Newton's Third Law of Motion: Newton's Third Law states that every action has an equal and opposite reaction. Action and reaction forces always act simultaneously on different objects.

Law of Conservation of Momentum: The total momentum of a system remains constant when no external force acts on it. During a collision, momentum is transferred from one object to another but the total momentum remains unchanged.

Everyday Applications: Seat belts protect passengers by reducing the effects of inertia during sudden stops. Rocket propulsion, gun recoil and walking are practical applications of Newton's Third Law of Motion.

flashcards.q: What is displacement?
flashcards.a: Displacement is the shortest straight-line distance between the initial and final positions of an object.

flashcards.q: What is acceleration?
flashcards.a: Acceleration is the rate of change of velocity with time.

flashcards.q: State Newton's First Law of Motion.
flashcards.a: An object remains at rest or continues in uniform motion unless acted upon by an external unbalanced force.

flashcards.q: What is momentum?
flashcards.a: Momentum is the product of the mass and velocity of an object. P = m × v.

flashcards.q: State the Law of Conservation of Momentum.
flashcards.a: The total momentum of a system remains constant when no external force acts on it.

flashcards.q: What is the difference between distance and displacement?
flashcards.a: Distance is the total path length travelled (scalar), while displacement is the shortest straight-line distance between start and end points (vector).

flashcards.q: What is uniform circular motion?
flashcards.a: Motion of an object along a circular path with constant speed, where velocity changes direction at every point.

practice.q: Write the three equations of motion.
practice.a: v = u + at | s = ut + ½at² | v² = u² + 2as

practice.q: What is the SI unit of force and how is it defined?
practice.a: The SI unit is Newton (N). One Newton is the force that produces an acceleration of 1 m/s² in a body of mass 1 kg.

practice.q: State Newton's Third Law and give one example.
practice.a: Every action has an equal and opposite reaction. Example: Rocket propulsion — gases are expelled downward and the rocket moves upward.

practice.q: What does the area under a velocity-time graph represent?
practice.a: The area under a velocity-time graph represents the distance travelled by the object during that time.

practice.q: Differentiate between balanced and unbalanced forces.
practice.a: Balanced forces do not change the motion of an object. Unbalanced forces produce a net force that changes the speed or direction of motion.

chapters.title: Work and Energy
notes: Work: Work is said to be done when a force applied on an object causes it to move in the direction of the force. If there is no displacement, no work is done even if a force is applied.
Conditions for Work to be Done: Work is done only when a force acts on an object and the object moves due to that force. If either force or displacement is absent, the work done is zero.
Formula for Work: Work = Force × Displacement. W = F × s, where W = Work, F = Force, s = Displacement. The SI unit of work is Joule (J).
Positive Work: Positive work is done when the force and displacement act in the same direction. The work done increases as the force or displacement increases.
Negative Work: Negative work is done when the force acts opposite to the direction of displacement. Friction is a common example because it opposes motion.

Zero Work: Zero work is done when there is no displacement or when the force acts perpendicular to the direction of motion. Carrying a school bag while walking on a level road is an example of zero work done by the upward force.
Energy: Energy is the capacity of an object to do work. Energy can neither be created nor destroyed but can only be converted from one form to another.
Forms of Energy: Energy exists in different forms such as mechanical, heat, light, sound, electrical and chemical energy. These forms can be transformed from one into another.
Mechanical Energy: Mechanical energy is the sum of kinetic energy and potential energy possessed by an object. It depends on the object's motion and position.
Kinetic Energy: Kinetic energy is the energy possessed by an object because of its motion. Formula: KE = ½mv², where m = Mass and v = Velocity.

Potential Energy: Potential energy is the energy possessed by an object due to its position or height above the ground. Formula: PE = mgh, where m = Mass, g = acceleration due to gravity and h = Height.
Transformation of Energy: Energy can change from one form to another without any loss of total energy. For example, a falling object converts potential energy into kinetic energy.
Law of Conservation of Energy: Energy can neither be created nor destroyed. The total amount of energy always remains constant during any physical process.
Power: Power is the rate at which work is done or energy is transferred. Formula: P = W/t, where P = Power, W = Work, t = Time. The SI unit of power is Watt (W).
SI Unit of Power: One watt is the power of a device that does one joule of work in one second.
Commercial Unit of Electrical Energy: Electrical energy used in homes is measured in kilowatt-hour (kWh). One kilowatt-hour = 3.6 × 10⁶ J.
Simple Machines: Simple machines help us perform work with less effort by changing the magnitude or direction of the applied force. They make work easier but do not reduce the total work done.
Lever: A lever is a rigid bar that rotates about a fixed point called the fulcrum. Depending on the positions of the fulcrum, load and effort, levers are classified into three types.
Mechanical Advantage: Mechanical advantage is the ratio of load lifted by a machine to the effort applied. Formula: MA = Load ÷ Effort. A higher MA means less effort is needed.

Efficiency of a Machine: Efficiency is the ratio of useful work obtained from a machine to the work supplied to it. No machine is 100% efficient because some energy is always lost due to friction. Formula: Efficiency = (Useful Work Output ÷ Work Input) × 100%.

Everyday Applications: Hydroelectric power plants convert the potential energy of stored water into electrical energy. Fans, mixers and electric motors convert electrical energy into mechanical energy.

flashcards.q: What is work?
flashcards.a: Work is done when a force causes an object to move in the direction of the applied force. W = F × s.

flashcards.q: What is kinetic energy?
flashcards.a: Kinetic energy is the energy possessed by an object due to its motion. KE = ½mv².

flashcards.q: State the Law of Conservation of Energy.
flashcards.a: Energy can neither be created nor destroyed but can only be converted from one form to another.

flashcards.q: What is power?
flashcards.a: Power is the rate at which work is done or energy is transferred. P = W/t.

flashcards.q: What is mechanical advantage?
flashcards.a: Mechanical advantage is the ratio of load lifted by a machine to the effort applied. MA = Load ÷ Effort.

flashcards.q: What is the commercial unit of electrical energy?
flashcards.a: The commercial unit of electrical energy is kilowatt-hour (kWh). 1 kWh = 3.6 × 10⁶ J.

flashcards.q: Give one example of energy transformation.
flashcards.a: A falling object converts potential energy (PE) into kinetic energy (KE).

practice.q: Write the formula for potential energy and explain each term.
practice.a: PE = mgh, where m = mass of object, g = acceleration due to gravity and h = height above the ground.

practice.q: Under what conditions is work done equal to zero?
practice.a: Work is zero when there is no displacement, or when the force acts perpendicular to the direction of motion.

practice.q: What is the difference between positive work and negative work?
practice.a: Positive work is done when force and displacement are in the same direction. Negative work is done when force acts opposite to displacement (e.g. friction).

practice.q: Why is no machine 100% efficient?
practice.a: Because some energy is always lost as heat due to friction between the moving parts of the machine.

practice.q: A device does 500 J of work in 10 seconds. Calculate its power.

chapters.title: Current Electricity
notes: Electric Charge: Electric charge is a fundamental property of matter responsible for electrical attraction and repulsion. There are two types: positive and negative.
Static Electricity: Static electricity is produced when electric charges accumulate on the surface of an object. It is commonly produced by rubbing two different materials together.

Electric Current: Electric current is the continuous flow of electric charges through a conductor. It flows only when a complete conducting path is available. Formula: I = Q/t, where I = Current, Q = Charge, t = Time. SI unit: Ampere (A).
Electric Circuit: An electric circuit is a closed path through which electric current flows from the source to the electrical components. If the circuit is broken, the current stops flowing.
Components of an Electric Circuit: A simple circuit consists of a cell, connecting wires, a switch and an electrical device such as a bulb. Each component performs a specific function.
Electric Cell: An electric cell converts chemical energy into electrical energy. It has two terminals: positive and negative.
Potential Difference: Potential difference is the work done to move a unit positive charge between two points. SI unit: Volt (V). It is measured using a voltmeter.
Resistance: Resistance is the property of a conductor that opposes the flow of electric current. It depends on the material, length, thickness and temperature of the conductor.
Conductors and Insulators: Conductors allow electric current to pass through them easily (e.g. copper, aluminium). Insulators do not allow current to pass (e.g. rubber, plastic, glass).

Ohm's Law: Current through a conductor is directly proportional to the potential difference across it at constant temperature. Formula: V = IR, where V = Potential Difference, I = Current, R = Resistance.
Resistance in Series: When resistors are connected one after another, total resistance = R₁ + R₂ + R₃ + ...
Resistance in Parallel: When resistors are connected across the same two points, 1/R = 1/R₁ + 1/R₂ + 1/R₃ + ... The equivalent resistance is always less than the smallest individual resistance.
Heating Effect of Electric Current: When current flows through a conductor, electrical energy converts into heat energy. This effect is used in electric irons, heaters and kettles.
Electric Fuse: An electric fuse is a safety device that protects appliances from excessive current by melting and breaking the circuit.
Miniature Circuit Breaker (MCB): An MCB is an automatic safety device that disconnects the circuit during overload or short circuit. Unlike a fuse, it can be reset and reused.
Short Circuit: A short circuit occurs when the live wire comes into direct contact with the neutral wire, causing a sudden increase in current and risk of fire.
Electromagnet: An electromagnet is a temporary magnet produced by passing electric current through a coil wound around a soft iron core. It loses magnetism when the current is switched off.
Magnetic Effect of Electric Current: A current-carrying conductor produces a magnetic field around it. This is used in electric bells, cranes and loudspeakers.
Fleming's Right-Hand Thumb Rule: When the thumb points in the direction of current, the curled fingers indicate the direction of the magnetic field around the conductor.
Safety While Using Electricity: Handle appliances with dry hands. Avoid damaged wires and overloaded sockets to prevent electric shock.

flashcards.q: What is electric current?
flashcards.a: Electric current is the continuous flow of electric charges through a conductor. I = Q/t.

flashcards.q: State Ohm's Law.
flashcards.a: Current is directly proportional to potential difference at constant temperature. V = IR.

flashcards.q: What is an electromagnet?
flashcards.a: A temporary magnet produced by passing electric current through a coil wound around a soft iron core.

flashcards.q: What is the function of an electric fuse?
flashcards.a: An electric fuse protects circuits by melting and breaking the circuit when excessive current flows.

flashcards.q: What is the magnetic effect of electric current?
flashcards.a: A current-carrying conductor produces a magnetic field around it.

flashcards.q: What is the difference between series and parallel resistance?
flashcards.a: In series: R = R₁ + R₂ + R₃. In parallel: 1/R = 1/R₁ + 1/R₂ + 1/R₃.

flashcards.q: What is potential difference?
flashcards.a: Work done to move a unit positive charge between two points. Measured in Volts (V).

practice.q: What is the difference between a fuse and an MCB?
practice.a: A fuse melts and must be replaced after use. An MCB is an automatic device that can be reset and reused after tripping.

practice.q: Write Ohm's Law and state its condition.
practice.a: V = IR. Condition: The temperature of the conductor must remain constant.

practice.q: What is a short circuit and how does it occur?
practice.a: A short circuit occurs when the live wire comes into direct contact with the neutral wire, causing a sudden very large current that can damage appliances or cause fire.

practice.q: Name three applications of the heating effect of electric current.
practice.a: Electric iron, electric heater and electric kettle.

practice.q: State Fleming's Right-Hand Thumb Rule.
practice.a: When the thumb of the right hand points in the direction of current, the curled fingers indicate the direction of the magnetic field around the conductor.

chapters.title: Measurement of Matter
notes: Matter: Matter is anything that has mass and occupies space. Everything around us is made up of matter.
Physical Classification of Matter: Matter is classified into solids, liquids and gases based on the arrangement of its particles.
Solid State: Solids have a definite shape and volume because particles are closely packed. Particles can only vibrate about their fixed positions.
Liquid State: Liquids have a definite volume but no definite shape. Their particles can move freely and they take the shape of the container.
Gaseous State: Gases have neither definite shape nor volume because particles are far apart. They spread out and fill the entire container.
Intermolecular Force: The force of attraction between the particles of matter. It is strongest in solids, weaker in liquids and weakest in gases.
Chemical Classification of Matter: Based on chemical composition, matter is classified into elements, compounds and mixtures.
Element: A pure substance made up of only one type of atom. It cannot be broken down by ordinary chemical methods.
Compound: A pure substance formed when two or more elements combine chemically in a fixed proportion. Its properties differ from those of its constituent elements.
Mixture: Formed when two or more substances are mixed physically without a chemical reaction. Components can be separated by physical methods.
Homogeneous Mixture: Has uniform composition throughout. Salt solution and sugar solution are examples.
Heterogeneous Mixture: Has non-uniform composition; components can often be seen separately. Sand mixed with water is an example.
Organic Compounds: Carbon-containing compounds found in living organisms. Examples include glucose, starch and proteins.
Inorganic Compounds: Generally do not contain carbon as their main element. Water, common salt and washing soda are examples.
Mass: The amount of matter present in an object. It remains the same regardless of location.
Weight: The force with which the Earth attracts an object due to gravity. Weight changes with changes in gravitational force.
Volume: The amount of space occupied by an object. Measured in cubic units such as m³ or cm³.
Density: Mass of a substance per unit volume. Formula: ρ = m/V. Substances with higher density have more mass in the same volume.
Measuring Volume of Irregular Solids: Measured using the water displacement method. The increase in water level equals the volume of the solid.
SI Units: Scientists use the International System of Units. Common SI units include metre (m), kilogram (kg), second (s) and cubic metre (m³).
Importance of Accurate Measurement: Accurate measurement ensures reliable experimental results and helps reduce errors during practical work.

flashcards.q: What is matter?
flashcards.a: Matter is anything that has mass and occupies space.

flashcards.q: What is the difference between an element and a compound?
flashcards.a: An element contains only one type of atom. A compound is formed by the chemical combination of two or more elements in a fixed proportion.

flashcards.q: What is density?
flashcards.a: Density is the mass of a substance per unit volume. ρ = m/V.

flashcards.q: What is a homogeneous mixture?
flashcards.a: A mixture that has uniform composition throughout, such as salt solution.

flashcards.q: How is the volume of an irregular solid measured?
flashcards.a: Using the water displacement method — the increase in water level equals the volume of the solid.

flashcards.q: What is the difference between mass and weight?
flashcards.a: Mass is the amount of matter in an object and does not change with location. Weight is the gravitational force acting on an object and changes with location.

flashcards.q: Name three states of matter and give one property of each.
flashcards.a: Solid — definite shape and volume. Liquid — definite volume but no definite shape. Gas — neither definite shape nor volume.

practice.q: A stone has a mass of 200 g and displaces 80 cm³ of water. Calculate its density.
practice.a: Density = Mass ÷ Volume = 200 ÷ 80 = 2.5 g/cm³.

practice.q: Differentiate between homogeneous and heterogeneous mixtures with examples.
practice.a: Homogeneous: uniform composition throughout. Example: salt solution. Heterogeneous: non-uniform composition. Example: sand and water.

practice.q: Why is intermolecular force strongest in solids?
practice.a: In solids, particles are very closely packed together, which results in very strong intermolecular forces of attraction.

practice.q: Name two measuring instruments and state what they measure.
practice.a: Metre scale — measures length. Measuring cylinder — measures volume of liquids.

practice.q: What is the difference between organic and inorganic compounds?
practice.a: Organic compounds mainly contain carbon (e.g. glucose). Inorganic compounds do not mainly contain carbon (e.g. common salt, water).

chapters.title: Acids, Bases and Salts
notes: Acids: Acids are substances that produce hydrogen ions (H⁺) when dissolved in water. They generally have a sour taste and turn blue litmus paper red.
Properties of Acids: Acids react with metals to produce hydrogen gas and react with bases to form salts and water. Strong acids ionize completely; weak acids ionize partially.
Common Acids: Hydrochloric acid (HCl), sulphuric acid (H₂SO₄) and nitric acid (HNO₃) are mineral acids. Citric acid in lemons and acetic acid in vinegar are natural acids.
Bases: Bases produce hydroxide ions (OH⁻) when dissolved in water. They usually have a bitter taste and feel soapy to touch.
Properties of Bases: Bases turn red litmus paper blue and neutralize acids to form salt and water. Strong bases are highly reactive.
Alkalis: Alkalis are bases that dissolve in water and produce hydroxide ions. Sodium hydroxide and potassium hydroxide are common examples.
Indicators: Substances that change colour in the presence of acids and bases to help identify whether a solution is acidic or basic.
Litmus Indicator: A natural indicator obtained from lichens. Blue litmus turns red in acids; red litmus turns blue in bases.
Phenolphthalein: Remains colourless in acidic solutions and turns pink in basic solutions.
Methyl Orange: Turns red in acidic solutions and yellow in basic solutions.
Universal Indicator: Shows different colours depending on the strength of an acid or base. Used to estimate the pH value.
pH Scale: Measures how acidic or basic a solution is. Ranges from 0 to 14. pH 7 = neutral, below 7 = acidic, above 7 = basic.
Neutralization Reaction: Occurs when an acid reacts with a base to form salt and water. General equation: Acid + Base → Salt + Water.
Salts: Ionic compounds formed during a neutralization reaction. Different acid-base combinations produce different salts.
Common Salts: Common salt (NaCl), baking soda and washing soda are widely used in daily life.
Uses of Acids: Used in manufacturing fertilizers, medicines, dyes and batteries, and in food preservation and industrial processes.
Uses of Bases: Used in making soaps, detergents, paper and medicines. Also used to neutralize acidic substances.
Everyday Applications: Antacids contain weak bases that neutralize excess stomach acid. Farmers add lime to acidic soil to improve fertility.

flashcards.q: What is an acid?
flashcards.a: An acid is a substance that produces hydrogen ions (H⁺) when dissolved in water.

flashcards.q: What is a base?
flashcards.a: A base is a substance that produces hydroxide ions (OH⁻) when dissolved in water.

flashcards.q: What is a neutralization reaction?
flashcards.a: A reaction in which an acid reacts with a base to form salt and water.

flashcards.q: What is the pH scale?
flashcards.a: The pH scale measures the acidity or basicity of a solution on a scale from 0 to 14. pH 7 = neutral.

flashcards.q: What are indicators?
flashcards.a: Substances that change colour to show whether a solution is acidic or basic.

flashcards.q: What is the difference between strong and weak acids?
flashcards.a: Strong acids ionize completely in water; weak acids ionize only partially.

flashcards.q: What are alkalis?
flashcards.a: Alkalis are bases that dissolve in water and produce hydroxide ions. Examples: NaOH, KOH.

practice.q: Write the general equation for a neutralization reaction.
practice.a: Acid + Base → Salt + Water. Example: HCl + NaOH → NaCl + H₂O.

practice.q: What colour does litmus paper show in an acidic and a basic solution?
practice.a: In acid: blue litmus turns red. In base: red litmus turns blue.

practice.q: What is the colour change of phenolphthalein and methyl orange in acids and bases?
practice.a: Phenolphthalein: colourless in acid, pink in base. Methyl orange: red in acid, yellow in base.

practice.q: Why are antacids used?
practice.a: Antacids contain weak bases that neutralize excess acid in the stomach, providing relief from acidity.

practice.q: How does lime help acidic soil?
practice.a: Lime (calcium hydroxide) is a base that neutralizes excess acid in the soil, making it suitable for farming.

chapters.title: Classification of Plants
notes: Classification of Plants: Plants are classified into different groups based on structure, method of reproduction and presence of vascular tissues. Classification makes scientific study systematic.
Cryptogams: Plants that reproduce by spores and do not produce flowers or seeds. They are generally simple in structure and grow in moist places.
Phanerogams: Plants that produce seeds and have well-developed reproductive organs. They are more advanced than cryptogams.
Thallophyta: The simplest plants whose body is not differentiated into roots, stems and leaves. Most are found in water and reproduce through spores.
Algae: Green, chlorophyll-containing organisms that prepare food by photosynthesis. Mainly aquatic and release oxygen into the environment.
Bryophyta: Small, non-vascular plants that grow in moist and shady places. Known as 'amphibians of the plant kingdom' because they require water for reproduction.
Pteridophyta: The first vascular plants with true roots, stems and leaves. Reproduce through spores instead of seeds.
Gymnosperms: Seed-producing plants whose seeds are not enclosed within fruits. Most are evergreen trees with needle-like leaves.
Angiosperms: Flowering plants whose seeds are enclosed within fruits. The largest and most diverse group of plants on Earth.
Monocotyledons: Flowering plants whose seeds contain one cotyledon. They have parallel leaf venation and fibrous root systems.
Dicotyledons: Flowering plants whose seeds contain two cotyledons. They have reticulate leaf venation and tap root systems.
Vascular Tissue: Consists of xylem and phloem that transport water, minerals and food throughout the plant.
Xylem: Transports water and dissolved minerals from roots to different parts of the plant. Also provides mechanical support.
Phloem: Transports food prepared in leaves to all parts of the plant.
Spore Formation: Tiny reproductive structures produced by cryptogams that germinate to form new plants under favourable conditions.
Seed Formation: Seeds are formed after fertilization and contain the embryo and stored food for new plant growth.
Importance of Plant Classification: Helps identify plants, understand evolutionary relationships and makes scientific study organized.
Economic Importance of Plants: Plants provide food, oxygen, medicines, timber, fibres and many useful products.
Differences — Cryptogams vs Phanerogams: Cryptogams reproduce by spores without flowers or seeds. Phanerogams reproduce by seeds with well-developed reproductive structures.

flashcards.q: What are cryptogams?
flashcards.a: Cryptogams are plants that reproduce by spores and do not produce flowers or seeds.

flashcards.q: What are angiosperms?
flashcards.a: Angiosperms are flowering plants whose seeds are enclosed within fruits.

flashcards.q: What is the function of xylem?
flashcards.a: Xylem transports water and minerals from the roots to different parts of the plant.

flashcards.q: What is the difference between monocotyledons and dicotyledons?
flashcards.a: Monocotyledons have one cotyledon and parallel venation. Dicotyledons have two cotyledons and reticulate venation.

flashcards.q: Why is plant classification important?
flashcards.a: It helps identify plants, understand their characteristics and makes scientific study easier.

flashcards.q: Why are bryophytes called amphibians of the plant kingdom?
flashcards.a: Because they need water for reproduction, just like amphibians need water for part of their life cycle.

flashcards.q: What are gymnosperms?
flashcards.a: Seed-producing plants whose seeds are not enclosed within fruits. Most are evergreen with needle-like leaves.

practice.q: List the main groups of cryptogams and give one example of each.
practice.a: Thallophyta (e.g. algae), Bryophyta (e.g. moss), Pteridophyta (e.g. fern).

practice.q: What is the difference between gymnosperms and angiosperms?
practice.a: Gymnosperms have seeds not enclosed in fruits (e.g. pine). Angiosperms have seeds enclosed in fruits (e.g. mango).

practice.q: What is the function of phloem?
practice.a: Phloem transports food (glucose) prepared in leaves to all other parts of the plant.

practice.q: How do monocots and dicots differ in root type and leaf venation?
practice.a: Monocots have fibrous roots and parallel venation. Dicots have tap roots and reticulate venation.

practice.q: Why are pteridophytes called the first vascular plants?
practice.a: Because they are the first group of plants to have xylem and phloem for transporting water and food.

chapters.title: Energy Flow in an Ecosystem
notes: Ecosystem: A functional unit where living organisms interact with one another and with their physical environment. Includes biotic and abiotic components.
Biotic Components: Living organisms in an ecosystem — producers, consumers and decomposers.
Abiotic Components: Non-living parts of an ecosystem such as air, water, sunlight, soil and temperature.
Producers: Green plants that prepare their own food by photosynthesis. They form the first trophic level and are the primary source of energy.
Consumers: Organisms that depend directly or indirectly on producers for food. Classified into primary, secondary and tertiary consumers.
Primary Consumers: Herbivores that feed directly on plants. Examples include deer, rabbits, grasshoppers and cows.
Secondary Consumers: Carnivores or omnivores that feed on primary consumers. They occupy the third trophic level.
Tertiary Consumers: Top carnivores that feed on secondary consumers. Found at the highest trophic level.
Decomposers: Organisms such as bacteria and fungi that break down dead plants and animals into simpler substances. They recycle nutrients back into the environment.
Food Chain: A sequence in which energy passes from one organism to another through feeding relationships. Always begins with producers.
Types of Food Chain: Grazing food chain and detritus food chain both help in the transfer of energy and nutrients.
Food Web: A network of interconnected food chains in an ecosystem. Provides multiple feeding relationships and increases ecosystem stability.
Trophic Levels: Each stage in a food chain. Energy decreases as it moves from one trophic level to the next.
Flow of Energy: Energy flows in one direction from the Sun to producers then to consumers. Unlike nutrients, energy is not recycled.
Ten Percent Law: Only about 10% of the energy at one trophic level is transferred to the next level. The remaining 90% is lost as heat.
Ecological Pyramid: A graphical representation of number, biomass or energy at different trophic levels. The pyramid of energy is always upright.
Nutrient Cycle: Nutrients move continuously between living organisms and the environment. These cycles maintain the balance of essential elements in nature.
Ecological Balance: Maintained when all living and non-living components exist in proper proportion. Disturbance in any component affects the entire ecosystem.
Conservation of Ecosystems: Protecting forests, reducing pollution and conserving biodiversity help maintain healthy ecosystems.

flashcards.q: What is an ecosystem?
flashcards.a: A functional unit where living organisms interact with one another and with their physical environment.

flashcards.q: Who are producers in an ecosystem?
flashcards.a: Producers are green plants that prepare their own food by photosynthesis.

flashcards.q: What is a food chain?
flashcards.a: A sequence through which energy passes from one organism to another by feeding, starting from producers.

flashcards.q: State the Ten Percent Law.
flashcards.a: Only about 10% of the energy at one trophic level is transferred to the next trophic level.

flashcards.q: What is the role of decomposers in an ecosystem?
flashcards.a: Decomposers break down dead organisms and recycle nutrients back into the environment.

flashcards.q: What is a food web?
flashcards.a: A network of interconnected food chains in an ecosystem that provides multiple feeding relationships.

flashcards.q: What is an ecological pyramid?
flashcards.a: A graphical representation of number, biomass or energy at different trophic levels of an ecosystem.

practice.q: Give an example of a food chain with four trophic levels.
practice.a: Grass → Grasshopper → Frog → Snake. (Grass is the producer; grasshopper is primary consumer; frog is secondary; snake is tertiary.)

practice.q: Explain why energy decreases at each trophic level.
practice.a: At each trophic level, most energy (about 90%) is lost as heat during life processes. Only 10% is available to the next level.

practice.q: What is the difference between a food chain and a food web?
practice.a: A food chain is a single sequence of energy flow. A food web is a network of multiple interconnected food chains.

practice.q: Why is the pyramid of energy always upright?
practice.a: Because energy always decreases as it moves from one trophic level to the next, giving a pyramid shape that narrows upward.

practice.q: Why are decomposers important for ecological balance?
practice.a: Decomposers break down dead organic matter, recycle nutrients back into the soil and clean the environment, maintaining ecological balance.

chapters.title: Useful and Harmful Microbes
notes: Microorganisms: Tiny living organisms that cannot be seen with the naked eye. Found in air, water, soil and inside living organisms.
Types of Microorganisms: Bacteria, fungi, protozoa, algae and viruses — each group has its own characteristics.
Bacteria: Unicellular organisms without a true nucleus. Reproduce rapidly by binary fission and may be useful or harmful.
Fungi: Eukaryotic microorganisms that absorb food from dead and decaying organic matter. Reproduce by spores and are important decomposers.
Protozoa: Single-celled organisms with a well-defined nucleus. Some are free-living; others cause diseases in humans and animals.
Algae: Chlorophyll-containing organisms that prepare food through photosynthesis. Produce oxygen and serve as food for aquatic organisms.
Viruses: Extremely small particles that reproduce only inside living host cells. Contain either DNA or RNA within a protein coat.
Useful Microorganisms in Food: Used to prepare curd, bread, cheese and fermented products. Yeast is used in baking; bacteria help make curd.
Microorganisms in Medicine: Certain bacteria and fungi produce antibiotics. Vaccines prepared using microorganisms protect against infectious diseases.
Microorganisms in Agriculture: Some bacteria fix atmospheric nitrogen into soil, improving crop productivity and reducing need for chemical fertilizers.
Decomposition: Bacteria and fungi break down dead plants and animals into simpler substances, recycling nutrients and keeping the environment clean.
Nitrogen Cycle: A natural process in which nitrogen is continuously recycled between the atmosphere, soil and living organisms.
Harmful Microorganisms: Some cause diseases in humans, animals and plants. They may also spoil food and damage useful materials.
Food Spoilage: Microorganisms grow on food under favourable conditions and cause it to spoil. Proper storage and preservation help prevent this.
Food Preservation: Process of preventing food from spoiling by controlling growth of microorganisms. Methods include drying, refrigeration, salting and pasteurization.
Diseases Caused by Bacteria: Cholera, typhoid, tuberculosis and pneumonia.
Diseases Caused by Viruses: Influenza, polio, measles, dengue and AIDS.
Diseases Caused by Protozoa: Malaria and amoebic dysentery — spread through contaminated food, water or insect vectors.
Prevention of Microbial Diseases: Personal hygiene, safe water, proper sanitation and vaccination help prevent many microbial diseases.
Importance of Vaccination: Vaccination prepares the body's immune system to fight disease-causing microorganisms.

flashcards.q: What are microorganisms?
flashcards.a: Microorganisms are tiny living organisms that can be seen only with the help of a microscope.

flashcards.q: Name the five main groups of microorganisms.
flashcards.a: Bacteria, fungi, protozoa, algae and viruses.

flashcards.q: How are microorganisms useful in agriculture?
flashcards.a: Some microorganisms fix atmospheric nitrogen in the soil and improve soil fertility.

flashcards.q: What is food preservation?
flashcards.a: Food preservation is the process of preventing food spoilage by controlling the growth of microorganisms.

flashcards.q: Why is vaccination important?
flashcards.a: Vaccination helps the body develop immunity and protects it from many infectious diseases.

flashcards.q: Name two diseases caused by viruses.
flashcards.a: Influenza and polio.

flashcards.q: What is the nitrogen cycle?
flashcards.a: A natural process where nitrogen is continuously recycled between the atmosphere, soil and living organisms.

practice.q: Give two examples each of useful and harmful microorganisms.
practice.a: Useful: Lactobacillus (for curd), Penicillium (for penicillin). Harmful: Vibrio cholerae (cholera), Plasmodium (malaria).

practice.q: Name four methods of food preservation.
practice.a: Drying, refrigeration, salting and pasteurization.

practice.q: What role do bacteria play in the nitrogen cycle?
practice.a: Nitrogen-fixing bacteria convert atmospheric nitrogen into compounds that plants can absorb, enriching the soil.

practice.q: How can microbial diseases be prevented?
practice.a: By maintaining personal hygiene, drinking clean water, using proper sanitation and getting vaccinated.

practice.q: Why do viruses require living host cells to reproduce?
practice.a: Viruses do not have their own cellular machinery for reproduction. They must enter living cells and use the host's cellular machinery to multiply.

chapters.title: Environmental Management
notes: Environment: Includes all the living and non-living things that surround us. Provides resources necessary for survival of all living organisms.
Environmental Management: Proper use, protection and conservation of natural resources to maintain ecological balance and support sustainable development.
Natural Resources: Materials obtained from nature that are useful to humans — air, water, soil, forests, minerals and wildlife.
Conservation of Natural Resources: Using natural resources wisely without wasting them to ensure availability for future generations.
Biodiversity: The variety of plants, animals and microorganisms found on Earth. Greater biodiversity helps maintain stable and healthy ecosystems.
Importance of Biodiversity: Provides food, medicine, raw materials and ecological stability. Helps maintain the balance of nature.
Pollution: Contamination of the environment by harmful substances. Affects health of living organisms and damages natural ecosystems.
Air Pollution: Caused by harmful gases, smoke and dust in the atmosphere. Leads to respiratory diseases, global warming and acid rain.
Water Pollution: Harmful chemicals, sewage or industrial waste in water bodies. Makes water unsafe for drinking and aquatic life.
Soil Pollution: Caused by excessive use of fertilizers, pesticides and improper waste disposal. Reduces soil fertility and crop production.
Noise Pollution: Excessive and unwanted sound from vehicles, industries and loudspeakers. Leads to stress, hearing problems and reduced concentration.
Solid Waste Management: Proper collection, segregation, recycling and disposal of waste materials to keep the environment clean.
Biodegradable Waste: Waste that can be broken down naturally by microorganisms. Examples: food waste, paper and dry leaves.
Non-biodegradable Waste: Waste that does not decompose naturally and remains for a long time. Examples: plastic, glass and metal.
Waste Segregation: Separating waste into biodegradable and non-biodegradable categories before disposal to improve recycling.
The 3Rs: Reduce, Reuse and Recycle — following these principles helps conserve resources and minimize waste generation.
Rainwater Harvesting: Collecting and storing rainwater for future use. Helps conserve water and recharge groundwater.
Afforestation: Planting trees in areas where forests did not previously exist. Increases green cover and reduces environmental problems.
Deforestation: Large-scale cutting of forests for agriculture, industries and development. Leads to soil erosion, loss of biodiversity and climate change.
Sustainable Development: Meeting present needs without affecting the ability of future generations to meet their own needs.

flashcards.q: What is environmental management?
flashcards.a: The proper use and conservation of natural resources to protect the environment.

flashcards.q: What are biodegradable wastes?
flashcards.a: Wastes that can be decomposed naturally by microorganisms, such as food waste and paper.

flashcards.q: What are the 3Rs of waste management?
flashcards.a: Reduce, Reuse and Recycle.

flashcards.q: What is sustainable development?
flashcards.a: Meeting present needs without harming the ability of future generations to meet their own needs.

flashcards.q: What is rainwater harvesting?
flashcards.a: The process of collecting and storing rainwater for future use.

flashcards.q: What is deforestation and what are its effects?
flashcards.a: Large-scale cutting of forests. Effects include soil erosion, loss of biodiversity and climate change.

flashcards.q: What is afforestation?
flashcards.a: Planting trees in areas where forests did not previously exist to increase green cover.

practice.q: Name the four main types of pollution and one cause of each.
practice.a: Air — vehicle exhaust. Water — industrial waste. Soil — excessive pesticide use. Noise — loudspeakers and traffic.

practice.q: Why is waste segregation important?
practice.a: Separating biodegradable and non-biodegradable waste makes recycling more efficient and reduces pollution.

practice.q: How does deforestation contribute to climate change?
practice.a: Forests absorb CO₂. When trees are cut, CO₂ levels rise, increasing the greenhouse effect and causing global warming.

practice.q: What is the difference between biodegradable and non-biodegradable waste?
practice.a: Biodegradable waste decomposes naturally (food waste, paper). Non-biodegradable waste does not decompose naturally (plastic, glass).

practice.q: Suggest three ways an individual can help protect the environment.
practice.a: Use cloth bags, conserve electricity and properly dispose of waste in segregated bins.

chapters.title: Information Communication Technology (ICT)
notes: Information and Communication Technology (ICT): The use of computers and communication systems to collect, process, store and share information. Important in education, business and daily life.
Information: Processed and meaningful data that helps in decision-making. Accurate information is essential for communication and problem-solving.
Communication: The process of exchanging information between people or devices. Modern communication has become faster and more efficient through ICT.
Computer: An electronic device that accepts data, processes it and produces useful information. Performs calculations quickly and stores large amounts of data.
Hardware: The physical parts of a computer that can be seen and touched. Examples: monitor, keyboard, mouse, CPU and printer.
Software: A set of programs and instructions that tell a computer how to perform tasks. Two types: system software and application software.
Input Devices: Used to enter data and instructions into a computer. Examples: keyboard, mouse, scanner and microphone.
Output Devices: Display or produce processed information from a computer. Examples: monitor, printer, speakers and projector.
Storage Devices: Used to store data and programs. Examples: hard disks, pen drives and memory cards.
Internet: A worldwide network that connects millions of computers. Allows users to access information and communicate from anywhere.
World Wide Web (WWW): A collection of web pages available through the Internet. Accessed using web browsers.
E-mail: An electronic method of sending and receiving messages over the Internet. One of the fastest communication tools.
Search Engine: A software service used to find information on the Internet. Helps users locate websites, images, videos and documents.
Digital Communication: Exchanging information using electronic devices and digital networks. Supports instant communication via emails, video calls and messaging.
Cyber Safety: Using the Internet responsibly and protecting personal information online. Strong passwords and avoiding unknown links help improve security.
Advantages of ICT: Improves communication, education, healthcare and business. Saves time, increases efficiency and provides easy access to information.
Disadvantages of ICT: Excessive dependence may lead to cybercrime, privacy issues and reduced physical activity.
Applications of ICT: Used in education, banking, healthcare, agriculture, industries and government services.
Everyday Applications: Online learning, digital banking, online shopping and video conferencing are common examples of ICT.

flashcards.q: What is Information and Communication Technology (ICT)?
flashcards.a: ICT is the use of computers and communication technologies to collect, process, store and share information.

flashcards.q: What is the Internet?
flashcards.a: The Internet is a worldwide network that connects millions of computers.

flashcards.q: What are input devices?
flashcards.a: Input devices are hardware used to enter data and instructions into a computer.

flashcards.q: What is cyber safety?
flashcards.a: Cyber safety means using the Internet responsibly and protecting personal information online.

flashcards.q: Name any two applications of ICT.
flashcards.a: ICT is used in education, healthcare, banking, agriculture and many other fields.

flashcards.q: What is the difference between hardware and software?
flashcards.a: Hardware is the physical parts of a computer. Software is the set of programs and instructions that run on the hardware.

flashcards.q: What is the World Wide Web?
flashcards.a: A collection of web pages available through the Internet, accessed using web browsers.

practice.q: Give two advantages and two disadvantages of ICT.
practice.a: Advantages: saves time, improves communication. Disadvantages: risk of cybercrime, reduced physical activity.

practice.q: Name three input and three output devices.
practice.a: Input: keyboard, mouse, scanner. Output: monitor, printer, speakers.

practice.q: What is cyber safety and why is it important?
practice.a: Cyber safety means using the Internet responsibly. It is important to protect personal data from hackers, cybercriminals and online fraud.

practice.q: How has ICT changed education?
practice.a: ICT enables online learning, digital textbooks, video lessons and instant access to educational resources from anywhere.

practice.q: What is the difference between system software and application software?
practice.a: System software controls the basic functions of a computer (e.g. operating system). Application software performs specific tasks for users (e.g. word processor).

chapters.title: Reflection of Light
notes: Light: A form of energy that enables us to see objects. It travels in a straight line and does not require a material medium.
Luminous and Non-luminous Objects: Luminous objects produce their own light (e.g. Sun, bulb). Non-luminous objects reflect light from other sources (e.g. Moon).
Reflection of Light: The bouncing back of light into the same medium after striking a surface. Most objects are visible because they reflect light.
Laws of Reflection: (1) The angle of incidence equals the angle of reflection. (2) The incident ray, reflected ray and normal at the point of incidence all lie in the same plane.
Plane Mirror: A smooth, flat reflecting surface. Forms an erect, virtual image of the same size as the object, as far behind the mirror as the object is in front.
Image Formed by a Plane Mirror: Virtual, erect and laterally inverted. Cannot be obtained on a screen.
Lateral Inversion: The apparent reversal of the left and right sides of an image in a plane mirror. Words appear reversed in a mirror.
Spherical Mirrors: Mirrors whose reflecting surface forms part of a sphere. Two types: concave mirror and convex mirror.
Concave Mirror: Has its reflecting surface curved inward. Can produce real or virtual images depending on the position of the object.
Convex Mirror: Has its reflecting surface curved outward. Always forms a virtual, erect and diminished image.
Pole (P): The centre of the reflecting surface of a spherical mirror.
Centre of Curvature (C): The centre of the sphere of which the mirror forms a part.
Radius of Curvature: The distance between the pole and the centre of curvature.
Principal Axis: The straight line passing through the pole and centre of curvature. Used as a reference line for ray diagrams.
Principal Focus (F): The point where rays parallel to the principal axis meet or appear to meet after reflection.
Uses of Concave Mirrors: Used in headlights, shaving mirrors, torches and solar cookers.
Uses of Convex Mirrors: Used as rear-view mirrors in vehicles because they provide a wider field of view.
Multiple Reflection: Light reflected several times between two mirrors. Used in kaleidoscopes and periscopes.

flashcards.q: What is reflection of light?
flashcards.a: Reflection is the bouncing back of light after striking a surface.

flashcards.q: State the laws of reflection.
flashcards.a: Angle of incidence = angle of reflection. Incident ray, reflected ray and normal lie in the same plane.

flashcards.q: What type of image is formed by a plane mirror?
flashcards.a: A plane mirror forms a virtual, erect and laterally inverted image.

flashcards.q: Why are convex mirrors used as rear-view mirrors?
flashcards.a: They provide a wider field of view and always form erect, diminished images.

flashcards.q: What is lateral inversion?
flashcards.a: The apparent interchange of the left and right sides of an image in a plane mirror.

flashcards.q: What is the principal focus of a mirror?
flashcards.a: The point where rays parallel to the principal axis meet or appear to meet after reflection.

flashcards.q: Name two uses of concave mirrors.
flashcards.a: Used in shaving mirrors and headlights of vehicles.

practice.q: State the two laws of reflection.
practice.a: (1) Angle of incidence = angle of reflection. (2) The incident ray, reflected ray and normal all lie in the same plane.

practice.q: What is the difference between a concave and a convex mirror?
practice.a: A concave mirror has its reflecting surface curved inward and can form real images. A convex mirror has its surface curved outward and always forms virtual, erect, diminished images.

practice.q: Why does a word appear reversed when seen in a plane mirror?
practice.a: Due to lateral inversion — a plane mirror swaps the left and right sides of an image.

practice.q: What is multiple reflection? Name two devices that use it.
practice.a: Multiple reflection occurs when light reflects several times between two mirrors. Devices: kaleidoscope and periscope.

practice.q: A concave mirror is used in a solar cooker. Why?
practice.a: A concave mirror converges parallel rays of sunlight to a focus, producing intense heat that can be used for cooking.

chapters.title: Study of Sound
notes: Sound: A form of energy produced due to vibrations of objects. Travels through a material medium as longitudinal waves.
Production of Sound: Sound is produced when an object vibrates. These vibrations create compressions and rarefactions in the surrounding medium.
Medium of Sound: Sound requires a material medium (solid, liquid or gas) to travel. It cannot travel through a vacuum.
Longitudinal Waves: Particles of the medium vibrate parallel to the direction of wave propagation. Sound waves in air are longitudinal waves.
Compression: A region where particles of the medium are close together, creating high pressure and high density.
Rarefaction: A region where particles of the medium are far apart, creating low pressure and low density.
Amplitude: Maximum displacement of vibrating particles from their mean position. Greater amplitude produces louder sound.
Frequency: The number of vibrations completed in one second. Measured in hertz (Hz).
Time Period: The time taken to complete one vibration. It is the reciprocal of frequency.
Wavelength: The distance between two successive compressions or two successive rarefactions. Represented by the Greek letter lambda (λ).
Speed of Sound: Depends on the nature and temperature of the medium. Travels fastest in solids and slowest in gases.
Audible Range: The human ear can hear sounds with frequencies between 20 Hz and 20,000 Hz.
Infrasonic Sound: Frequency below 20 Hz. Produced by earthquakes, elephants and some natural phenomena.
Ultrasonic Sound: Frequency above 20,000 Hz. Used in medical imaging, cleaning delicate objects and industrial testing.
Echo: The repetition of sound caused by reflection from a distant surface. Heard when reflected sound reaches the ear after a short interval.
Reverberation: Persistence of sound due to repeated reflections inside a closed space. Excessive reverberation reduces clarity.
Applications of Ultrasound: Used for medical diagnosis, detecting cracks in metals and measuring sea depth.
Noise Pollution: Excessive and unwanted sound in the environment. Causes stress, hearing loss and sleep disturbances.
Control of Noise Pollution: Plant trees, use silencers and avoid unnecessary use of loudspeakers.

flashcards.q: What is sound?
flashcards.a: Sound is a form of energy produced due to vibrations of objects.

flashcards.q: Can sound travel through a vacuum?
flashcards.a: No. Sound requires a material medium for propagation.

flashcards.q: What is frequency?
flashcards.a: Frequency is the number of vibrations completed in one second. Unit: hertz (Hz).

flashcards.q: What is an echo?
flashcards.a: An echo is the repetition of sound caused by reflection from a distant surface.

flashcards.q: What are ultrasonic waves?
flashcards.a: Sound waves with frequencies greater than 20,000 Hz.

flashcards.q: What is the difference between compression and rarefaction?
flashcards.a: Compression is a region of high pressure where particles are close together. Rarefaction is a region of low pressure where particles are far apart.

flashcards.q: Name three applications of ultrasound.
flashcards.a: Medical imaging (sonography), detecting cracks in metals and measuring depth of the sea.

practice.q: What is the audible range of the human ear?
practice.a: 20 Hz to 20,000 Hz.

practice.q: In which medium does sound travel fastest and why?
practice.a: Sound travels fastest in solids because the particles are closely packed and transmit vibrations more quickly.

practice.q: What is the difference between echo and reverberation?
practice.a: An echo is a reflected sound heard after a distinct interval. Reverberation is the persistence of sound due to multiple rapid reflections within a closed space.

practice.q: Why can sound not travel in a vacuum?
practice.a: Sound is a mechanical wave that requires particles of matter to propagate. In a vacuum, there are no particles to carry the vibrations.

practice.q: How is infrasonic sound different from ultrasonic sound?
practice.a: Infrasonic: frequency below 20 Hz, produced naturally. Ultrasonic: frequency above 20,000 Hz, used in medicine and industry.

chapters.title: Carbon – An Important Element
notes: Carbon: A non-metallic element that forms the basis of most organic compounds. Found in living organisms, fuels and many natural substances.
Properties of Carbon: Carbon is tetravalent — it forms four covalent bonds with other atoms. This allows it to form a large number of stable compounds.
Catenation: The property of carbon atoms to bond with one another and form long chains, branched chains or rings. This results in millions of carbon compounds.
Allotropes of Carbon: Different forms of the same element with different structures and properties. Diamond, graphite and fullerene are the main allotropes.
Diamond: The hardest naturally occurring substance made of carbon atoms. Used in jewellery and cutting or drilling tools.
Graphite: A soft, black allotrope of carbon with layers of atoms. Good conductor of electricity. Used in pencils, lubricants and electrodes.
Fullerene: A spherical allotrope of carbon with atoms arranged in a cage-like structure. Used in nanotechnology and medicine.
Organic Compounds: Compounds that mainly contain carbon and hydrogen. Form the basis of food, fuels, medicines and many industrial products.
Hydrocarbons: Organic compounds made only of carbon and hydrogen atoms. Main components of fuels such as petrol, diesel and natural gas.
Saturated Hydrocarbons: Contain only single covalent bonds between carbon atoms. Called alkanes and are generally less reactive.
Unsaturated Hydrocarbons: Contain one or more double or triple bonds between carbon atoms. Classified as alkenes and alkynes.
Covalent Bond: Formed when two atoms share electrons with each other. Carbon mainly forms covalent compounds.
Properties of Covalent Compounds: Generally have low melting and boiling points. Most do not conduct electricity because they lack free ions.
Fuels: Substances that release heat energy when they burn. Coal, petroleum, natural gas and LPG are commonly used fuels.
Fossil Fuels: Formed from remains of plants and animals buried millions of years ago. Important energy sources but non-renewable.
Uses of Carbon Compounds: Used in medicines, plastics, textiles, fertilizers and fuels.
Environmental Effects: Burning fossil fuels releases CO₂ and pollutants. Excessive use contributes to global warming and air pollution.
Conservation of Fossil Fuels: Should be used carefully as they are limited. Using renewable energy helps conserve them.

flashcards.q: Why is carbon called a tetravalent element?
flashcards.a: Carbon is called tetravalent because it forms four covalent bonds with other atoms.

flashcards.q: What is catenation?
flashcards.a: Catenation is the property of carbon atoms to bond with one another and form long chains or rings.

flashcards.q: Name any three allotropes of carbon.
flashcards.a: Diamond, graphite and fullerene.

flashcards.q: What are hydrocarbons?
flashcards.a: Hydrocarbons are compounds made only of carbon and hydrogen atoms.

flashcards.q: Why should fossil fuels be conserved?
flashcards.a: Fossil fuels are non-renewable resources and will eventually be exhausted if used excessively.

flashcards.q: What is the difference between saturated and unsaturated hydrocarbons?
flashcards.a: Saturated hydrocarbons (alkanes) have only single bonds. Unsaturated hydrocarbons (alkenes/alkynes) have double or triple bonds.

flashcards.q: What is a covalent bond?
flashcards.a: A bond formed when two atoms share electrons with each other.

practice.q: What are the properties of diamond that make it useful?
practice.a: Diamond is the hardest natural substance, making it useful for cutting and drilling tools and for jewellery.

practice.q: How is graphite different from diamond, even though both are allotropes of carbon?
practice.a: Diamond is the hardest substance and does not conduct electricity. Graphite is soft, slippery and a good conductor of electricity.

practice.q: Why do carbon compounds form such a large variety?
practice.a: Because of catenation (carbon-carbon bonding) and tetravalency (four bonds possible), allowing chains, branches and rings of different lengths.

practice.q: What are fossil fuels? Give two examples.
practice.a: Fossil fuels are formed from ancient plant and animal remains. Examples: coal and petroleum.

practice.q: How does burning fossil fuels affect the environment?
practice.a: It releases CO₂ and other pollutants, causing air pollution, acid rain and contributing to global warming.

chapters.title: Substances in Common Use
notes: Substances in Daily Life: Many substances are used in homes, industries and agriculture. These substances improve quality of life and support human activities.
Soap: A cleansing agent prepared by the reaction of fats or oils with an alkali. It removes dirt and grease by forming micelles.
Detergent: A synthetic cleansing agent that works effectively even in hard water. Widely used for washing clothes and utensils.
Difference Between Soap and Detergent: Soap does not produce much lather in hard water because it forms scum. Detergents produce good lather in both soft and hard water.
Hard Water: Contains dissolved calcium and magnesium salts. Reduces the cleaning efficiency of soap.
Soft Water: Does not contain significant calcium and magnesium salts. Forms lather easily with soap.
Baking Soda: Sodium hydrogen carbonate (NaHCO₃). Used in baking, as an antacid and in fire extinguishers.
Washing Soda: Sodium carbonate (Na₂CO₃·10H₂O). Used for cleaning, softening hard water and in manufacture of glass and soaps.
Bleaching Powder: Used for disinfecting drinking water and bleaching cotton and paper. Also used as a germicide.
Plaster of Paris: Obtained by heating gypsum. Used for making statues, casts, toys and false ceilings.
Cement: An important building material used in construction of houses, bridges and roads. Hardens when mixed with water.
Glass: Manufactured by heating silica with suitable chemicals at high temperatures. Used in windows, bottles, lab equipment and optical instruments.
Ceramics: Hard, heat-resistant materials prepared by heating clay at high temperatures. Used for tiles, pots, sanitary ware and electrical insulators.
Polymers: Large molecules formed by joining many small molecules called monomers. Plastics, nylon and polyester are common examples.
Plastics: Lightweight, durable and water-resistant materials. Excessive use causes environmental pollution.
Synthetic Fibres: Man-made fibres such as nylon, polyester and acrylic. Strong, durable and easy to maintain.
Advantages of Synthetic Materials: Lightweight, strong, inexpensive and resistant to corrosion.
Environmental Concerns: Many synthetic materials are non-biodegradable and remain in the environment for a long time.
Safe Use of Chemicals: Household chemicals should be used according to safety instructions. Proper storage prevents accidents.

flashcards.q: What is soap made of?
flashcards.a: Soap is made by the reaction of fats or oils with an alkali.

flashcards.q: Why do detergents work better than soap in hard water?
flashcards.a: Detergents do not form scum with calcium and magnesium ions in hard water, so they produce good lather and clean effectively.

flashcards.q: What is baking soda? What is its chemical name?
flashcards.a: Baking soda is sodium hydrogen carbonate (NaHCO₃). Used in baking, as an antacid and in fire extinguishers.

flashcards.q: What is Plaster of Paris and how is it prepared?
flashcards.a: Plaster of Paris is prepared by heating gypsum. It is used for casts, statues, toys and false ceilings.

flashcards.q: What are polymers?
flashcards.a: Large molecules formed by joining many small repeating molecules called monomers. Examples: plastics, nylon.

flashcards.q: What is the difference between hard water and soft water?
flashcards.a: Hard water contains dissolved calcium and magnesium salts and reduces soap lather. Soft water lacks these salts and lathers easily.

flashcards.q: Name two uses of washing soda.
flashcards.a: Used for cleaning and for softening hard water.

practice.q: Name two uses each of baking soda and washing soda.
practice.a: Baking soda: baking bread, neutralizing stomach acid. Washing soda: cleaning clothes, softening hard water.

practice.q: Why is soap less effective in hard water?
practice.a: Hard water contains Ca²⁺ and Mg²⁺ ions that react with soap to form insoluble scum instead of lather, reducing its cleaning action.

practice.q: What are synthetic fibres? Give two examples.
practice.a: Man-made fibres produced from chemicals. Examples: nylon and polyester.

practice.q: Why do synthetic materials raise environmental concerns?
practice.a: Most synthetic materials like plastic are non-biodegradable. They remain in the environment for hundreds of years and pollute land, water and air.

practice.q: What is bleaching powder used for?
practice.a: Bleaching powder is used to disinfect drinking water, bleach cotton and paper and as a germicide.

chapters.title: Life Processes in Living Organisms
notes: Life Processes: The basic activities performed by living organisms to maintain life — nutrition, respiration, transportation, excretion, growth and reproduction.
Nutrition: The process by which organisms obtain and utilize food for energy, growth and repair. Essential for survival.
Autotrophic Nutrition: Organisms prepare their own food using sunlight, CO₂ and water. Green plants are autotrophs performing photosynthesis.
Heterotrophic Nutrition: Organisms depend on other organisms for food. Animals, fungi and many microorganisms are heterotrophs.
Photosynthesis: Green plants prepare food in the presence of sunlight and chlorophyll. Equation: CO₂ + H₂O → Glucose + O₂ (Sunlight and Chlorophyll required).
Respiration: Breaking down food to release energy required for life activities. Takes place continuously in cells of all living organisms.
Aerobic Respiration: Occurs in the presence of oxygen and releases a large amount of energy. Produces CO₂ and water as end products.
Anaerobic Respiration: Occurs in the absence of oxygen and releases less energy. Observed in yeast and certain bacteria.
Transportation in Plants: Water and minerals transported through xylem; food is transported through phloem.
Xylem: Carries water and dissolved minerals from roots to leaves. Also provides mechanical support.
Phloem: Transports food prepared in leaves to all parts of the plant to support growth and food storage.
Transportation in Humans: The circulatory system transports oxygen, nutrients and hormones to all body parts. Also carries waste to excretory organs.
Heart: A muscular organ that pumps blood throughout the body, maintaining continuous circulation.
Blood: A connective tissue that transports oxygen, nutrients, hormones and waste materials. Protects against infections.
Excretion: The process of removing metabolic waste products from the body to maintain internal balance.
Human Excretory System: Consists of kidneys, ureters, urinary bladder and urethra. Removes nitrogenous wastes and excess water.
Transpiration: Loss of water as water vapour from aerial parts of plants. Helps cool the plant and aids upward transport of water.
Importance of Life Processes: Ensure survival, growth and reproduction. Without them, living organisms cannot sustain life.

flashcards.q: What are life processes?
flashcards.a: Basic activities that maintain life in living organisms: nutrition, respiration, transportation, excretion, growth and reproduction.

flashcards.q: What is photosynthesis?
flashcards.a: The process by which green plants prepare food using sunlight, CO₂ and water. O₂ is released as a by-product.

flashcards.q: What is the function of xylem?
flashcards.a: Xylem transports water and minerals from the roots to different parts of the plant.

flashcards.q: What is respiration?
flashcards.a: Respiration is the process of releasing energy from food in cells.

flashcards.q: What is excretion?
flashcards.a: Excretion is the process of removing metabolic waste products from the body.

flashcards.q: What is the difference between aerobic and anaerobic respiration?
flashcards.a: Aerobic respiration uses oxygen and releases more energy. Anaerobic respiration occurs without oxygen and releases less energy.

flashcards.q: What is transpiration?
flashcards.a: The loss of water in the form of water vapour from the aerial parts of plants.

practice.q: Write the equation for photosynthesis.
practice.a: Carbon dioxide + Water → Glucose + Oxygen (in the presence of sunlight and chlorophyll).

practice.q: What are the components of the human excretory system?
practice.a: Kidneys, ureters, urinary bladder and urethra.

practice.q: How does transpiration help plants?
practice.a: Transpiration cools the plant and helps draw water and minerals up from the roots to leaves.

practice.q: Distinguish between autotrophic and heterotrophic nutrition.
practice.a: Autotrophs prepare their own food using sunlight (e.g. plants). Heterotrophs depend on other organisms for food (e.g. animals).

practice.q: What is the role of the heart in transportation?
practice.a: The heart pumps blood throughout the body, ensuring continuous circulation of oxygen, nutrients and waste materials.

chapters.title: Heredity and Variation
notes: Heredity: The transmission of characteristics from parents to their offspring. Ensures that many traits are passed from one generation to the next.
Variation: Differences in characteristics among individuals of the same species. Helps organisms adapt to changing environments.
Importance of Variation: Increases chances of survival of a species under changing conditions. Forms the basis of evolution through natural selection.
Genetics: The branch of biology that studies heredity and variation. Explains how traits are inherited from parents to offspring.
Gregor Mendel: Known as the Father of Genetics because of his experiments on pea plants. His work established the basic principles of inheritance.
Traits: Observable characteristics or features of an organism. May be inherited from parents or influenced by the environment.
Genes: Units of heredity present on chromosomes. Carry the information responsible for specific traits.
Chromosomes: Thread-like structures found in the nucleus of cells. Contain genes made up of DNA.
DNA (Deoxyribonucleic Acid): The genetic material that stores hereditary information. Controls development and functioning of living organisms.
Dominant Traits: Expressed even when only one copy of the gene is present. Masks the expression of the recessive trait.
Recessive Traits: Expressed only when both copies of the gene are recessive. Remains hidden in the presence of a dominant trait.
Mendel's Monohybrid Cross: Involves inheritance of one pair of contrasting traits. Mendel observed a 3:1 ratio in the F₂ generation.
Evolution: Gradual change in living organisms over many generations. Results in development of new species from earlier forms.
Fossils: Preserved remains or traces of ancient organisms found in rocks. Provide important evidence for evolution.
Adaptation: The process by which organisms develop features that help them survive in their environment.
Natural Selection: The process by which organisms with favourable traits survive and reproduce more successfully. Plays an important role in evolution.
Importance of Heredity: Maintains continuity among generations while allowing useful variations. Helps scientists understand inheritance and genetic disorders.
Everyday Applications: Knowledge of heredity used in agriculture, animal breeding and medicine to develop improved crops and better disease management.

flashcards.q: What is heredity?
flashcards.a: Heredity is the transmission of characteristics from parents to offspring.

flashcards.q: Who is known as the Father of Genetics?
flashcards.a: Gregor Johann Mendel is known as the Father of Genetics.

flashcards.q: What are genes?
flashcards.a: Genes are the units of heredity that carry information for specific traits.

flashcards.q: What is variation?
flashcards.a: Variation refers to the differences in characteristics among individuals of the same species.

flashcards.q: Why are fossils important?
flashcards.a: Fossils provide evidence about the evolution and history of ancient organisms.

flashcards.q: What is natural selection?
flashcards.a: The process by which organisms with favourable traits survive and reproduce more successfully, driving evolution.

flashcards.q: What is the difference between dominant and recessive traits?
flashcards.a: A dominant trait is expressed when one or two copies are present. A recessive trait is expressed only when two recessive copies are present.

practice.q: What is a monohybrid cross? What ratio did Mendel observe in F₂?
practice.a: A cross involving one pair of contrasting traits. Mendel observed a 3:1 ratio of dominant to recessive traits in the F₂ generation.

practice.q: What is the role of chromosomes in heredity?
practice.a: Chromosomes carry genes (made of DNA) that contain hereditary information passed from parents to offspring during reproduction.

practice.q: How is adaptation related to natural selection?
practice.a: Organisms with adaptations (favourable traits) survive and reproduce better. Over time, natural selection preserves these traits in the population.

practice.q: Why is DNA called the genetic material?
practice.a: DNA stores all hereditary information and controls the development, functioning and characteristics of living organisms.

practice.q: How is knowledge of heredity useful in agriculture?
practice.a: It helps develop high-yielding, disease-resistant crop varieties through selective breeding and genetic engineering.

chapters.title: Introduction to Biotechnology
notes: Biotechnology: The branch of science that uses living organisms, cells or their products to develop useful products and technologies. Combines biology with technology to improve human life.
Importance of Biotechnology: Applications in agriculture, medicine, industry and environmental protection. Helps increase food production, improve healthcare and reduce pollution.
Genetic Engineering: The process of changing the genetic material of an organism to obtain desired characteristics. Allows scientists to transfer useful genes from one organism to another.
Gene: The basic unit of heredity that carries genetic information and determines inherited characteristics.
Recombinant DNA Technology: Combines DNA from different organisms to produce useful products. Widely used in medicine and agriculture.
Genetically Modified Organisms (GMOs): Organisms whose genes have been altered using biotechnology. Developed to improve disease resistance, yield and other qualities.
Tissue Culture: The technique of growing plant cells or tissues under sterile laboratory conditions. Produces many identical plants in a short time.
Advantages of Tissue Culture: Allows rapid production of healthy, disease-free plants. Used for large-scale cultivation of economically important crops.
Biotechnology in Agriculture: Develops high-yielding, pest-resistant and drought-resistant crop varieties. Improves productivity and reduces crop losses.
Biotechnology in Medicine: Used to produce medicines such as insulin, vaccines and antibiotics. Helps in diagnosing and treating many diseases.
Biotechnology in Industry: Uses microorganisms to produce enzymes, organic acids, vitamins and fermented products. Makes industrial production faster and more efficient.
Environmental Biotechnology: Helps clean polluted environments using microorganisms through bioremediation. Reduces environmental pollution naturally.
Biofertilizers: Preparations containing beneficial microorganisms that improve soil fertility by increasing nutrient availability. Reduce the need for chemical fertilizers.
Biopesticides: Biological agents used to control harmful insects and pests. Environmentally friendly and safer than chemical pesticides.
Ethical Issues in Biotechnology: Biotechnology should be used responsibly to ensure safety of humans and the environment. Ethical guidelines help prevent misuse of genetic technologies.
Advantages of Biotechnology: Improves food production, healthcare, environmental conservation and industrial processes.
Limitations of Biotechnology: Requires advanced technology, skilled professionals and careful regulation. Improper use may lead to environmental and ethical concerns.

flashcards.q: What is biotechnology?
flashcards.a: Biotechnology is the use of living organisms or their products to develop useful products and technologies.

flashcards.q: What is genetic engineering?
flashcards.a: The process of modifying the genes of an organism to obtain desired characteristics.

flashcards.q: What is tissue culture?
flashcards.a: The technique of growing plant cells or tissues under sterile laboratory conditions.

flashcards.q: What are biofertilizers?
flashcards.a: Preparations containing beneficial microorganisms that improve soil fertility.

flashcards.q: Name any two applications of biotechnology.
flashcards.a: Biotechnology is used in agriculture (GM crops), medicine (insulin production), industry and environmental protection.

flashcards.q: What are GMOs?
flashcards.a: Genetically Modified Organisms are organisms whose genes have been altered using biotechnology.

flashcards.q: What is bioremediation?
flashcards.a: The use of microorganisms to clean polluted environments naturally.

practice.q: What is recombinant DNA technology? Give one application.
practice.a: It involves combining DNA from different organisms. Application: production of human insulin using bacteria.

practice.q: What are the advantages of tissue culture?
practice.a: It allows rapid production of many identical, healthy, disease-free plants from a single parent plant.

practice.q: How does biotechnology help in agriculture?
